FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data; 11,880 ABCD participants at baseline, passive sensing via ABCD-specific EARS application

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: describes and validates passively-sensed smartphone keyboard and app use measures from EARS data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Scientific Reports (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures smartphone use (EARS-derived keystroke and app use) but pairs it only with demographic variables (sex, operating system, age). No individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, etc.) on the other side of any analysis. No SM/phone-as-IV or SM/phone-as-DV analysis with trait-level outcome

notes: Validation/measurement-focused paper; smartphone measures are the focus but are not linked to an individual-trait outcome

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper describes NowIKnowMyABCD, a resource for the U.S. ABCD Study (11,000+ youth)

FT_IC5.evidence: Non-empirical paper: describes a documentation and communication resource platform (website, discussion board) for ABCD researchers. No original empirical analysis of ABCD data is reported. This is a resource/infrastructure paper

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data; N=11,868 participants followed from ages 9/10 to 13/14 across multiple waves

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multilevel logistic regression examining longitudinal patterns of technology subtypes (social media, video games, YouTube, texting, TV, video chat) and their associations with covariates

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures technology subtypes including social media, texting, and video chat. However, all outcome variables in the reported analyses are demographic/contextual: biological sex, parent education, household income, parental marital status. No individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, academic, etc.) appears as IV or DV paired with SM/phone use. Associations are technology-use-by-sociodemographic only

notes: Excluded under FT-EC2 (SM/phone paired only with demographic/contextual variables, not individual-trait-level outcomes)
